item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this and think it's very underrated. I actually haven't read 910 yet, so I think this does stand on its own.

+ My interpretation
– hide block

I got the impression that the O5 Council tried to erase this office and its occupant from existence, along with all related records, but that it didn't quite take thanks to the strength of the occupant's will to be remembered. Seems the coverup was still successful enough though, since there's no way to know any specifics from this article. It makes me wonder, how much stuff like this is there in the SCPverse that we just don't know about because the Council usually completely succeeds at coverups?

Also, I love that there's Foundation-issue bourbon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heads up, I fixed a small grammar error in the letter. If I'm the one in the wrong, feel free to revert. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you here, this is a great article without even needing to rely on other articles, 910 just serves to further show the point, like an example of the "oops."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I strongly disagree (though I would be the one inclined to.) I finished 3791 about a month before 910 was done, and it was my jumping-off point for the idea in the first place. There's even some divergence in what's being told between the two articles. 910 certainly provides some additional information, but I don't think reading it is necessary to appreciate or understand this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand what's going on, I think this is neat, but I don't like how 3791 absolutely requires reading 910 to understand. I have no problems with one SCP expanding on another, but I strongly feel that every main lister should be able to stand on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what else to call it? It's an occurrence where time isn't working in a way that would be described as normal (setting aside the fact that time is kind of wonky in the first place.) That seems like a pretty cut and dry example of a temporal anomaly to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certain particulars seem to rub off the wrong way; namely the description of this as a "temporal anomaly". That's usually used to mean time is flowing faster or slower (or even backwards) in a certain area, this is more things being restored to a specific point in history (in a way that depends on completely arbitrary human-defined dates).

Also, I'm not seeing any sort of coherent pattern here. You need at least a hint of rhyme to imply that there's a reason, even if there isn't enough to be certain what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To elaborate further on the background to this piece, one of my goals in writing this was to capture a more realistic aftermath of a coverup as opposed to a note inserted into a document that says "here's how were covered stuff up." As said before, I believe the answers to what's going on are contained within the piece, but I understand if people don't want to sit around digging through it for an extended period of time.

To a certain extent though, I don't think a specific truth of what was covered up is necessary for the piece. It's more about the things surrounding that event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there was some sort of big cover up I guess? It's hard to tell what's being covered up though and why what I assume the are O5 council are so intent on hiding it. Also I assume the Foundation personnel seen outside the window are meant to be the MTF mentioned in the Containment Procedures? It's never really explained what that had to do with anything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like the vagueness of this article. The depiction of the room, the storm outside and the armed personnel, and the contents of the room personally I found it vivid. It made me specifically think of a tense and quiet hour before some huge event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You left out enough information that I got a sense of something seriously wrong going on, and that was enough for my upvote, but what's left doesn't really seem cohesive to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd argue that the first three of your questions have answers that can be intuited from the text of the piece, or at least the absences of certain text. If it ends up being overly vague, that's on me as the author, but I did my best to provide details that would offer some explanation without spelling everything out.

I will say that the presence of that LOI is the thing I'm least sure of, and is at least partially just me being self-satisfyingly self-referential. There was an intended purpose to that too, but I don't really want to spell stuff out when it comes to the story taking place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you thought the story was interesting. While there was more I wanted to say about it, I ran up against the problem of plausibility a lot when considering what details could be added. The writing and editing process was a lot of balancing between making some details seem innocent vs. giving them enough meaning that the story didn't turn out overly vague.

Not having something goofy going on was a new experience for me too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly I feel like this is unfinished. It's not bad by any means, but nothing going on here seems to be explained as far as I can tell. Even the small things (why do they think people having perfect memory of a given hour on November 3rd is such a huge security/mental health risk, for example?) seem to be left hanging. What does the cognitohazard do? Why aren't Alpha-1 allowed in there? What's this howling pillar thing? You get the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a really interesting story here in the little details, but I feel like there's considerably more to this than is actually contained in the article.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
I'm genuinely unsure how this is going to be received by people in general, so this is going to be an adventure for everyone involved. I heard some interesting theories about what's going on here during the editing process.

Thanks to the following people for vary degrees of assistance with this:
The Great Hippo
MrWrong
Orsinia
Noxfero

Sorry if I'm forgetting anyone.
